text: The LORD told Abram to leave his country, his relatives, and his father's house for a land he would show him.
God promised to make him a great nation, bless him, make his name great, and make him a blessing.
God said he would bless those who blessed Abram and curse those who cursed him, and all families of the earth would be blessed through him.
So Abram left, as the LORD had told him, and Lot went with him; Abram was seventy-five when he left Haran.
Abram took Sarai his wife, his nephew Lot, all their possessions, and the people they had acquired in Haran, and set out for Canaan.
Abram passed through the land to the site of Shechem, to the oak of Moreh; the Canaanites were in the land at that time.
The LORD appeared to Abram and said he would give this land to his offspring, so Abram built an altar there.
From there he moved to the hill country east of Bethel, pitched his tent between Bethel and Ai, built another altar, and called on the LORD's name.
Then Abram journeyed on, still heading toward the Negev.
A severe famine struck the land, so Abram went down to Egypt to live there for a while.
As he neared Egypt, Abram told Sarai she was so beautiful he feared for his life.
He worried the Egyptians would kill him and let her live once they saw she was his wife.
He asked her to say she was his sister, so it would go well for him because of her.
When Abram arrived in Egypt, the Egyptians did notice how beautiful Sarai was.
Pharaoh's officials praised her to Pharaoh, and she was taken into his palace.
Pharaoh treated Abram well because of her, giving him sheep, oxen, donkeys, servants, and camels.
But the LORD struck Pharaoh's household with serious plagues because of Sarai.
Pharaoh summoned Abram and demanded to know why he hadn't said she was his wife.
Pharaoh asked why Abram had called her his sister, causing him to take her as a wife; now Pharaoh returned her and told Abram to go.
Pharaoh's men sent Abram away, along with his wife and everything he owned.
